summary.text: The Illinois Department of Transportation proposed widening Harlem Avenue (Illinois Route 43) from a two-lane road to a four-lane divided highway with a 16-foot median, spanning approximately 3 miles from 119th Street to 143rd Street in Cook County, Illinois. The project primarily affected the City of Palos Heights and surrounding communities. The improvement was needed to address traffic congestion and safety concerns on an inadequate two-lane road serving as a major north-south arterial through the Chicago metropolitan area, with intense urbanization expected to increase traffic demands. Anticipated benefits included reduced congestion, improved traffic flow, and channelized intersections for safer turning movements. Negative impacts included the acquisition of approximately 3 acres from the Cook County Forest Preserve District's 5,280-acre Tinley Creek Division, diminishing its value for natural resource preservation. The Forest Preserve District objected to characterizing the land as insignificant but agreed to accept 4.8 acres of replacement land. Flooding concerns near 127th Street and Navajo Creek were also raised by citizens, though the project's culvert improvements were not expected to fully resolve existing downstream flooding issues. No significant noise impacts were anticipated at the time of construction.

alternatives_proposed.name: No Action (Alternative A / Not to improve Harlem Avenue)
alternatives_proposed.description: Not to improve Harlem Avenue between 119th Street and 143rd Street. This alternative was rejected as inconsistent with national, state, and regional transportation planning goals and would leave a gap in the area transportation system, creating adverse effects of congestion and accidents.

alternatives_proposed.name: Alternative B - Improve Harlem Avenue (Alignment 1: New Right-of-Way)
alternatives_proposed.description: Provide an adequate cross-section for Harlem Avenue on entirely new right-of-way between 119th Street and 143rd Street, rather than utilizing the existing alignment.

alternatives_proposed.name: Alternative B - Improve Harlem Avenue (Alignment 2: Existing Right-of-Way - Recommended)
alternatives_proposed.description: Utilize the existing right-of-way and improve the existing roadway along its present alignment, requiring approximately 3 acres of acquisition from the Cook County Forest Preserve District, offset by 4.8 acres of replacement land. This was the recommended alignment.

alternatives_proposed.name: Variation A (Recommended)
alternatives_proposed.description: Improve Harlem Avenue to a four-lane divided highway with a sixteen-foot median along the existing alignment, with channelized intersections at 121st and 125th Streets and limited access (right-turn-in/right-turn-out only) at 122nd, 123rd, and 124th Streets.

alternatives_proposed.name: Variation B
alternatives_proposed.description: Improve Harlem Avenue to four lanes with a sixteen-foot median and add parallel frontage roads along each side between 121st and 125th Streets, with access to Harlem Avenue only through the frontage roads and channelized intersections at 121st and 125th Streets.

alternatives_proposed.name: Variation C
alternatives_proposed.description: Relocate Harlem Avenue away from the Palos Heights shopping area to a parallel alignment close to 72nd Court, with limited access at several intersections, and convert the existing right-of-way between 122nd and 124th Streets into a combined shoppers mall and parking area.

alternatives_proposed.name: Variation D
alternatives_proposed.description: Remove the Harlem Avenue improvement from the shopping area using a split alignment with southbound traffic west of the shopping area and northbound traffic east, with signalized intersections at 123rd Street and channelized intersections at 121st and 125th Streets.

alternatives_proposed.name: Variation E
alternatives_proposed.description: Remove the Harlem Avenue improvement from the shopping area by depressing the roadway approximately twenty feet and decking the air-rights to provide a shoppers mall above.

alternatives_proposed.name: Variation A-1
alternatives_proposed.description: Improve Harlem Avenue along its existing alignment with frontage roads and a slightly depressed profile, with the profile near existing grade at cross streets to accommodate channelized intersections; otherwise identical to Variation A.

chunks.title: Combined Final Environmental/Section 4(f) Statement for Harlem Avenue Improvement (Illinois Route 43)
chunks.description: This document presents a comprehensive environmental impact assessment for a proposed highway improvement project on Harlem Avenue in Cook County, Illinois, extending 3.0 miles from 119th Street to 143rd Street. The statement addresses the proposed four-lane divided highway design, environmental impacts including noise and water drainage, alternatives considered, and consultation with federal, state, and local agencies.

Description of Project The proposed area of improvement extends along Harlem Avenue (Illinois Route 43) from 119th Street on the north to 143rd Street on the south, a distance of approx- imately 3.0 miles. The existing roadway consists of two lanes, one 11 foot lane in each direction. The shoulders are gravel and earthen. The existing highway right-of-way is 66 feet from 135th Street south to 143rd Street and 100 feet, north of 135th Street. The proposed improvement will consist of four lanes, two 12 foot lanes in each direction, separated by a sixteen foot median. The roadway will have curb and gutter through- out and drainage will be provided by a storm sewer system. The improvement of Harlem Avenue will require an additional 34 feet of right-of-way from 135th Street south to 143rd Street and no additional right-of-way north of 135th Street. Intersections that will experience substantial traffic in- crease in the future, due to intense urbanization, will be channelized and signalized. The use of a sixteen foot median will allow protection and storage for left turning vehicles at the intersections. The improvement is proposed for construction in 1975. Environmental Impacts III. A. Lead to a noticeable Change in the Ambient Noise Level for a Substantial Number of People: No significant alteration in the existing ambient noise level is anticipated as a result of the improvement of Harlem Avenue based on Variation "A" and traffic at the time of construction. The existing profile of Harlem Avenue will not be signi- ficantly altered from its present profile. Therefore, vehicle noise associated with acceleration and decelera- tion on grades will be negligible. We expect a substantial reduction in the noise from motor vehicles as the existing traffic congestion in the area eases after the completion of this improvement. Noise problems associated with congested intersections will be minimized by the channelization of the inter- sections. The ambient noise level of the projected traffic along the improvement in the 1990 design year could have probable adverse impact, but the effect is not considered signi- ficant. B. Water Pollution, Drainage and Flooding: A major area of concern expressed by citizens at the public hearing involved the flooding problem in the area of 127th Street. The major water course in this area is Navajo Creek. The pattern of flooding upstream and downstream shows every indication *hat downstream restrictions are the probable cause of the flooding problem in the area. The pattern of flooding does not indicate whether or not any particular culverts are adequate or inadequate. A culvert will be provided at 127th Street near Harlem Avenue. The culvert will have sufficient capacity to handle the design runoff. The adequacy of the two culverts at Harlem Avenue and 127th Street would result in little or no relief to the flooding of land adjacent to Navajo Creek. C. Destruction, Derogation and Adverse Environmental Effects to Section 4(f) Land (Cook County Forest Preserve Dis- trict, Tinley Creek Division): In a letter sent to the Forest Preserve District of Cook County, Illinois they were asked to determine if the land (3 acres) required for highway purposes is of little or no value, not significant, to the remaining property (5,280 \ { acres}). The Forest Preserve District response set forth the following two points: 1. That such a statement as "little or no value" is anti-thesis to the Forest Preserve law and that they have difficulty in accepting the terminology required for Federal Administrative review. 2. The effect of the right-of-way taking - the taking will be used only to provide backslopes consistent with current design and safety criteria - is the diminished value of the tract for the preservation of natural resources. The diminishing aspect of construction to provide back- slopes cannot be denied, but such diminishing character- istics can be rectified by the use of timely plantings of proper vegetation in landscaping the project. IV. Alternatives The following alternatives of proposed action for the improvement of Harlem Avenue are available. A. Not to improve Harlem Avenue between 119th Street and 143rd Street. To improve Harlem Avenue between 119th Street and В. 143rd Street. Two probable alignments are possible between 119th Street and 143rd Street, they are: 1. Alignment - 1 To provide an adequate cross-section for Harlem Avenue on new right-of-way. 2. Alignment - 2 Utilization of the existing right-of-way and im- provement of the existing roadway. Even with the use of existing right-of-way some new right-of-way acquisition will be needed. Property will be required from the Cook County Forest Preserve District. The property acquisition, of 3 acres, will have a diminishing effect to the remaining property, but will not have the severe environmental degradation that relocation of the alignment between the established termini would have on the Forest Preserve lands. The proposed acquisition of 3 acres of land will be offset by replacement land for the Forest Preserve District in the amount of 4.8 acres. The general objectives of the Harlem Avenue improvement C. involves the consideration of the needs of the community of Palos Heights as well as the motoring public. Six alignment variations of the recommended alignment were studied for the Palos Heights area between 119th Street and 125th Street. The Variations of the recommended alignment: Variation "A" ....consists of improving Harlem Avenue to a four lane highway, divided by a sixteen foot median, along the existing alignment. The profile would be close to the existing grade line. The intersections at 121st Street and 125th Street will be channelized. Access at 122nd, 123rd, and 124th Street will be limited to right-turn-in and right-turn-out traffic movements only. Variation "B" .... Consists of improving Harlem Avenue to four lanes, divided by a sixteen foot median, with a parallel frontage road along each side of Harlem Avenue. The frontage roads will have their terminal points on Harlem Avenue immediately south of 121st Street and just north of 125th Street. Access will not be provided between 122nd Street, 123rd Street and 124th Street to Harlem Avenue except through the frontage roads. The inter- sections at 121st and 125th Street with Harlem Avenue will be channelized. Variation "C" ....provides for the relocation of existing Harlem Avenue away from the Palos Heights shopping area. With this proposed alignment relocated Harlem Avenue would be parallel, and close to 72nd Court. The intersections at 121st Street, 122nd Street, 124th Street will be limited to right-turn-in and right-turn-out traffic movements only. The existing right-of-way between 122nd and 124th Street would then be utilized as a combined shoppers mall and parking area. Variation "D" ....Would also remove the proposed Harlem Avenue improve- ment from the shopping area. There would be a split alignment with southbound vehicle traffic to the west of the shopping area and northbound vehicle traffic to the east of the shopping area. Signalized intersections would be provided for both northbound and southbound Harlem Avenue roadways at 123rd Street. The intersection at 121st and 125th Street will be channelized while the intersection at 122nd and 124th Street will be elimin ted. Variation "E" ....represents an attempt to remove the Harlem Avenue improvement from the shopping area by depressing the roadway approximately twenty feet and decking the air- rights to provide a shoppers mall. Variation "A-1" ....would improve Harlem Avenue along its existing align- ment with frontage roads and a slightly depressed pro- file. The profile will be near the existing grade at the cross streets in order to accommodate channelization of the intersections. The general improvement of Harlem Avenue and the cross streets is identical to Variation "A". Recommended Alignment Therefore, the recommended alignment alternative is, Alignment - 2, to utilize the existing right-of-way where feasible and to improve Harlem Avenue along its present alignment between 119th Street and 143rd Street. Recommended Variation Therefore, VARIATION "A" in conjunction with the recom- mented alignment alternative (utilizing existing right- of-way for improvement) is proposed for the improvement of Harlem Avenue between 119th Street and 143rd Street. A DESCRIPTION OF THE PROPOSED HIGHWAY IMPROVEMENT AND ITS SURROUNDINGS A. Existing Facility The proposed area of improvement extends along Harlem Avenue (Illinois Route 43) from 119th Street on the north to 143rd Street on the south, a distance of approximately 3.0 miles. The existing roadway consists of two lanes, one 11 foot lane in each direction. The shoulders are gravel and earthen. The existing highway right-of- way is 66 feet from 135th Street south to 143rd Street and 100 feet, north of 135th Street. (See Appendix A, Exhibit 1 ) The improvement of Harlem Avenue from 143rd Street south has been constructed as a four lane divided highway with a sixteen foot median. Harlem Avenue north of 119th Street is presently under design study for improvement. A new bridge has been constructed across the Calumet Sag Channel, the northern termini, with four lanes and a median. (See Appendix A, Exhibit 2 ) B. Land Use Existing land use along Harlem Avenue varies from public land on the south to residential and strip commercial on the north. The Cook County Forest Preserve District owns land on both sides of Harlem Avenue from south of 143rd Street north to 131st Street. Residential development extends north from 131st Street to 124th Street and from 121st Street to 119th Street. Commercial development is centered around the intersection with 127th Street and intense strip commercial development extends along Harlem Avenue from 124th Street to 121st Street. (See Appendix A , Exhibit 3 ) The only municipality involved with this improvement is the City of Palos Heights. chunks.description: This excerpt presents demographic and land use data for the Harlem Avenue improvement project area, including 1970 residential densities, population statistics across four townships, and existing traffic volumes. It also describes the affected environment including waterways, land uses, and baseline conditions in the study area.

The main channel of Tinley Creek does not cross Harlem Avenue within the limits of the improve- ment; but three tributary branches do cross Harlem Avenue, immediately north of 135th Street and immediately north of 143rd Street. One of the historical gauges of an area's vitality and its mobility relating to the transportation of people, goods, and services is its population. Since Harlem Avenue lies along the boundaries of four townships in the area of the improvement and is a major north-south transportation link, the following population data provides a glimpse into the vitality and mobility of the area. Existing The annual average 24 hour traffic volumes (1970 values) on Harlem Avenue are approximately: 14,100 vehicles at 119th Street N. vehicles at 135th Street 8,000 vehicles at 143rd Street Projected The annual average 24 hour traffic volumes projected for the year 1990 on Harlem Avenue are: 22,000 vehicles 119th Street at 20,000 vehicles at 135th Street 18,000 vehicles 143rd Street at Generated The anticipated new trips generated two years after completion of the Harlem Avenue improvement is esti- mated to be approximately 15% of the 1970 AADT. Proposed Facility E. The proposed improvement will consist of four lanes, two 12 foot lanes in each direction, separated by a sixteen foot median. The roadway will have curb and gutter throughout and drainage will be provided by a storm sewer system. The proposed typical cross-section is shown in Appendix A , Exhibit 4 . The improvement is proposed for construction in 1975. The improvement of Harlem Avenue will require an additional 34 feet of right-of-way from 135th Street south to 143rd Street and no additional right-of-way north of 135th Street. Intersections that will experience substantial traffic in- crease in the future, due to intense urbanization, will be channelized and signalized. The use of a sixteen foot median will allow protection and storage for left turning vehicles at the intersections. (See Appendix A, Exhibits 5 & 6.) The intersections to be improved are as follows: 119th Street - channelized 2. 120th Street - channelized but limited to right-turn-out and right turn-in traffic movements only. 3. 121st Street - channelized, Harlem only. 4. 122nd Street - channelized but limited to right-turn-out and right- turn-in traffic movements only. 123rd Street - channelized 124th Street - channelized but limited to right-turn-out and right- turn-in traffic movements only. 7. 125th Street - channelized; left-turn bays Harlem only. 8. 126th Street - channelized. 9. 127th Street - channelized. 10. 131st Street - partial channelization 11. 135th Street - channelized. 12. 143rd Street - already channelized as part of completed improvement south of 143rd Street. Various - Entrances; to Forest Preserve channelized as per request by the Forest Preserve District, and subdivisions. The drainage structure, a box culvert, on Harlem Avenue at Navajo Creek has sufficient capacity and will not be enlarged. A new culvert for Navajo Creek will be pro- vided at 127th Street, east of Harlem Avenue. A Section 4(f) Approval will be required for that portion of the improvement which passes through the Cook County Forest Preserve Property between 131st Street and 143rd Street. THE PROBABLE IMPACT OF THE PROPOSED DEVELOPMENT OR IMPROVEMENT A. Controversial issues, problems and objections to action likely to be highly controversial on environmental grounds: There is no action relative to this project that is likely to be highly controversial on environmental grounds. No objections to the project were directly addressed to the environment from the local governmental officials or citizens in attendance at the public hearing on March 19, 1970 at the Palos East School Auditorium. Have a significant aesthetic or visual effect: в. This project will have a significant POSITIVE effect upon the aesthetics of the area. The improvement of the roadway will result in the elimination of deterio- rating pavement and shoulders. The existing earthen ditch drainage system will be replaced with curb and gutter and a closed storm drainage system. C. Lead to significant increased air or water pollution in a given area: This improvement will not cause any significant increase in air or water pollution in a given area. Surface drainage will be provided so that there will be no significant effect upon existing drainage courses. All drainage from the improvement will be directed into a closed storm drainage system. We can expect a reduction in the air polluting emission from motor vehicles as the existing traffic congestion at intersections eases as a result of the increased capacity of intersections. Federal emissions standards for engines will also provide a reduction in air pollution. Qualitative estimates based on quanitative data available from the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency and the U.S. Department of Health Education and Welfare relating to vehicle emissions can be developed. This emission data can be developed with existing as well as projected traffic volumes. Calculations on vehicle emissions for a highway improvement can be summarized as follows: "Federal standards for new vehicles will cause a decrease in HC and CO emissions beyond 1980 in spite of the increase in vehicle population. Nitrogen oxide emis- sions, however, will continue to increase at a rate augmented by efforts to control CO and HC emissions, unless NOX emissions are specifically controlled."1 The above statement was made based on standards for emissions established through 1971. Since then specific actions, through issuance of standards through 1976, have been taken in regard to NOx emissions.2 These standards provide that NOX emissions will also be reduced even though vehicle population increases. Divide or disrupt an established community or divide D. existing land uses: No division of the established community or existing land uses will occur. Any disruption will be minimized by providing and maintaining continuous access along the entire improvement especially at commercial, business and residential areas during construction. The improvement will not divide the established com- munity or existing land uses because the improvement will be constructed on right-of-way already committed for highway purposes through the community of Palos Heights. 1. Control Techniques for Carbon Monoxide, Nitrogen Oxide, and Hydrocarbon Emissions from Mobile Sources. U.S. Dept. Health, Education and Welfare, (Washington, D.C.: National Air Pollution Control Administration, 1970): Publication No. AP-66, p. XV. 2. Federal Register; Volume 36, Number 128, Friday, July 2, 1971, Part II; (Title 45, Chapter XII, Part 1201.21), p. 12658. E. Displace significant number of people: Since the improvement will be provided on the existing alignment there will be no displacement of people as a direct result of this project. There will be no building acquisition required. There will be no building acquisition required for the improvement with Variation "A" and improvement of Harlem Avenue along the existing alignment. F. Have any effect on areas of unique interest or scenic beauty: There are no known areas of unique interest or scenic beauty associated with the improvement of Harlem Avenue. There are no known archeold ical or historic sites involved with this project. G. Substantially alter the pattern of behavior for a species, and interfere with important breeding, nesting, or feeding grounds: The improvement of Harlem Avenue is not involved with any known nesting, bracking or feeding grounds for wildlife. It will not interfere with the pattern of behavior for any known species in the area. The closest wildlife refuge, on Cook County Forest Preserve property, is approximately 1 1/2 miles east of the Harlem Avenue improvement, immediately north of 143rd Street. ANY PROBABLE ADVERSE ENVIRONMENTAL EFFECTS WHICH CANNOT III. BE AVOIDED SHOULD THE PROPOSAL BE IMPLEMENTED A. Lead to a noticeable Change in the Ambient Noise Level for a Substantial Number of People: No significant alteration in the existing ambient noise level is anticipated as a result of the improvement of Harlem Avenue based on Variation "A" and traffic at the time of construction. The existing profile of Harlem Avenue will not be signi- ficantly altered from its present profile. Therefore, vehicle noise associated with acceleration and decelera- tion on grades will be negligible. We expect a substantial reduction in the noise from motor vehicles as the existing traffic congestion in the area eases after the completion of this improvement. Noise problems associated with congested intersections will be minimized by the channelization of the inter- sections. The ambient noise level of the projected traffic along the improvement in the 1990 design year could have probable adverse impact, but the effect is not considered significant. B. Water Pollution, Drainage and Flooding: A major area of concern expressed by citizens at the public hearing involved the flooding problem in the area of 127th Street. The major water course in this area is Navajo Creek. Navajo Creek flows through the City of Palos Heights, Illinois. The headwaters for Navajo Creek are near 131st Street and 78th Avenue. The creek flows northeasterly for a distance of approximately 3½ miles to its outfall at the Calumet-Sag Channel near 119th Street and Ridgeland Avenue. Consulting the "Hydrologic Investigations Atlas HA-145", for the Palos Park quadrangle, published by the U.S. Geological Survey revealed that the watershed area involved in this study for the drainage of Harlem Avenue consists of an upstream watershed west of Harlem Avenue (815 acres) and one southwest of 127th Street (1,060 acres). Approxi- mately 210 acres of the watershed is on Cook County Forest Preserve District lands and the remaining acreage is either developed, or will be developed in the near future. The pattern of flooding for a major portion of the creek's length indicates that the entire channel cross section, culverts and underground conduits, may be inadequate to handle the storm runoff from the watershed. The pattern of flooding upstream and downstream shows every indication that downstream restrictions are the probable cause of the flooding problem in the area. The pattern of flooding does not indicate whether or not any particular culverts are adequate or inadequate. The existing drainage structure for Navajo Creek at Harlem Avenue is a 25 foot by 7 foot box culvert. The capacity of the culvert is sufficient to handle the watershed runoff, based on current culvert design criteria. Inspection of the culvert showed that it is almost completely filled with silt which further sup- ports evidence that downstream conditions are restricting the flow of water. When downstream restrictions occur water is ponded, its velocity reduces toward zero and deposition of suspended particles and silt occurs. A culvert will be provided at 127th Street near Harlem Avenue. The culvert will have sufficient capacity to handle the design runoff. The adequacy of the two culverts at Harlem Avenue and 127th Street would result in little or no relief to the flooding of land adjacent to Navajo Creek. C. Destruction, Derogation and Adverse Environmental Effects to Section 4(f) Land (Cook County Forest Preserve District, Tinley Creek Division): In a letter sent to the Forest Preserve District of Cook County, Illinois they were asked to determine if the land (3 acres) required for highway purposes is of little or no value, not significant, to the remaining property (5,280 acres). (See Appendix A & B, Exhibit 7 & 7 respectively) The Forest Preserve District response set forth the following two points: That such a statement as "little or no value" is anti-thesis to the Forest Preserve law and that they have difficulty in accepting the terminology required for Federal Administrative review. 2. The effect of the right-of-way taking - the taking will be used only to provide backslopes consistent with current design and safety cri- teria - is that the value of the tract is diminished for the preservation of natural rescurces. The diminishing aspect of construction to provide back- slopes cannot be denied, but such diminishing characteris- tics can be rectified by the use of timely plantings of proper vegetation in landscaping the project. IV. ALTERNATIVES The following alternatives of proposed action for the improvement of Harlem Avenue are available. A. Not to improve Harlem Avenue between 119th Sireet and 143rd Street. The non-improvement alternative is characterized as and results in the following: 1. Non-improvement of Harlem Avenue would not alleviate present traffic congestion between 119th Street and 131st Street, nor would it avoid future congestion. 2. It would not eliminate or reduce the present accident potential at intersections. It would not avoid the increase in accidents projected to accompany the increased traffic volumes projected through the year 1990. 3. It would not eliminate or decrease the increasing cost of maintaining the existing pavement and structures. The pavement and structures are deteriorating at a rapid, expensive, and unsafe rate. 4. Non-improvement of this section of Harlem Avenue would also increase air and noise pollution as a result of increased traffic loading and slower movement of vehicles on a cross-section with inadequate capacity. 5. Harlem Avenue is a major north-south highway. Failure to improve the highway to accommodate projected traffic requirements would reduce the cross-county mobility of many residents, in southern Cook County, as well as impedence of goods and services. To improve Harlem Avenue between 119th Street and в. 143rd Street. Two probable alignments are possible between 119th Street and 143rd Street: Alignment - 1 To provide an adequate cross-section for Harlem . Avenue on new right-of-way. This would be econo- mically sound or environmentally feasible for the following reasons: a. Relocation would cause severe damage to the Cook County Forest Preserve District property; which flanks Harlem Avenue from 131st Street, south to 151st Street and from about 76th Avenue on the west to Central Avenue on the east. (See Appendix A, Exhibit 8) b. Relocation would not comply with the goals, objectives and recommendations of various com- prehensive plans (Palos Heights, Cook County, Northeastern Illinois Planning Commission, Cook County Forest Preserve District), and regional and local transportation studies. c. Relocation would not minimize the probable detrimental impact to community values, the environmental and the mobility of people, goods and services. d. Intersection capacity and safety at inter- sections could be equally provided for along the existing right-of-way. e. No benefit in continuity of roadway facility or improved area access would be achieved by relocation. Alignment - 2 Utilization of the existing right-of-way and improve- ment of the existing roadway. This would be econo- mically and environmentally feasible for the following reasons: a. It would accommodate the 1990 design traffic volumes. b. The improvement would comply with the goals, objectives and recommendations of the various local and regional comprehensive plans and transportation studies. c. The improvement would minimize any detrimental impact to community values and open land preservation. Improve intersection capacity and d. safety. e. Provide for and maintain continuity of facility. f. Even with the use of existing right-of-way some new right-of-way acquisition will be needed. Property will be required from the Cook County Forest Preserve District. The property acquisition, of 3 acres, will have a deminishing effect to the remaining pro- property, but will not have the severe environmental degradation that relocation of the alignment between the established termini would have on the Forest Preserve lands. The proposed acquisition of 3 acres of land will be offset by replacement land for the Forest Preserve District in the amount of 4.8 acres. (See Appendix A , Exhibit 9 and Appendix B - Section 4(f)) Recommended Alignment: Therefore, the recommended alignment alternative is, Alignment - 2, to utilize the existing right-of-way where feasible and to improve Harlem Avenue along its present alignment between 119th Street and 143rd Street. C. The general objectives of the Harlem Avenue improvement involves the consideration of the needs of the community of Palos Heights as well as the motoring public. Six alignment variations of the recommended alignment were studied for the Palos Heights area between 119th Street and 125th Street. The variations of the recommended alignment are: Variation "A" ....consists of improving Harlem Avenue to a four lane highway, divided by a sixteen foot median, along the existing alignment. The profile would be close to the existing grade line. The intersections at 121st Street and 125th Street will be channelized. Access at 122nd and 124th Street will be limited to right-turn-in and right-turn-out traffic movements only. Variation "B" ....consists of improving Harlem Avenue to four lanes, divided by a sixteen foot median, with a parallel frontage road along each side of Harlem Avenue. The frontage roads will have their terminal points on Harlem Avenue immediately south of 121st Street and just north of 125th Street. Access will not be provided between 122nd Street, 123rd Street and 124th Street to Harlem Avenue except through the frontage roads. The inter- sections at 121st and 125th Street with Harlem Avenue will be channelized. Variation "C" ....provides for the relocation of existing Harlem Avenue away from the Palos Heights shopping area. With this proposed alignment relocated Harlem Avenue would be parallel, and close to 72nd Court. The intersections at 121st Street, 122nd Street, 124th Street will be limited to right-turn-in and right-turn-out traffic movements only. The existing right-of-way between 122nd and 124th Street would then be utilized as a combined shoppers mall and parking area. Variation "D" ....would also remove the proposed Harlem Avenue improve- ment from the shopping area. There would be a split alignment with southbound vehicle traffic to the west of the shopping area and northbound vehicle traffic to the east of the shopping area. Signalized intersections would be provided for both northbound and southbound Harlem Avenue roadways at 123rd Street. The intersection at 121st and 125th Street will be channelized while the intersection at 122nd and 124th Street will be eliminated. Variation "E" ....represents an attempt to remove the Harlem Avenue improvement from the shopping area by depressing the roadway approximately twenty feet and decking the air- rights to provide a shoppers mall. Variation "A-1" ....would improve Harlem Avenue along its existing align- ment with frontage roads and a slightly depressed profile. The profile will be near the existing grade at the cross streets in order to accommodate channelization of the intersections. The general improvement of Harlem Avenue and the cross streets is identical to Variation "A". 1. The impacts of these variations centered around the disruption of community values. Community impact was the prime consideration in evaluating the proposed variations. Variation "C" resulted in significant adverse environmental impact to the residential section of Palos Heights. Variation "B", "D", and A-1, were about equally balanced in significant adverse impact to residen- tial and commercial sectors. Variation "E" represented a drastic step in mini- mizing disruption to commercial and residential sectors. This variation did not rally much support from the community. The economic investment involved represented a considerable monetary burden on a community of some 10,000 persons. Variation "A" rallied support from both commercial and residential sectors and provided minimial dis- ruption to the Palos Heights business area. 2. Displacement of persons or property through the Palos Heights area is summarized below: Single Family Commercial Variation Residential or Business Variation "A" None None Variation "B" Variation "C" None Variation "D" Variation "E" None None Variation "A-1" Combined 3. At the public hearing held on March 19, 1970 at the Palos East School Auditorium, all six align- ment variations were formally presented. The response of those citizens attending the hearing was unanimously in favor of either Variation "A" or "A-1". The resi- dents of Palos Heights were determined and reaffirmed their resistance to relocating Harlem Avenue through the residential areas in order to avoid the shopping area. The city officials and the merchants affirmed that they would accept Variation "A-1", if the proposed frontage roads could be coordinated with a parking provision along the frontage of the stores between 122nd and 124th Streets. With the cooperation of the City and consideration of parking, a recommended plan was formulated. The final result of discussion and cooperation on the various alternates provided a plan with a frontage road parking area and a roadway profile very near the existing grade of the present facility. All of the original controversial impacts have been resolved. The improvement will follow the alignment described in Variation "A". (See Appendix A , Exhibit 5 & 6) Recommended Variation Therefore VARIATION "A" in conjunction with the recom- mented alignment alternative (utilizing existing right- of-way for improvement) is proposed for the improvement of Harlem Avenue between 119th Street and 143rd Street. v. THE RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN LOCAL SHORT-TERM USES OF MAN'S ENVIRONMENT AND THE MAINTENANCE AND ENHANCEMENT OF LONG- TERM PRODUCTIVITY Within this planning area the land-use would be altered regardless of a transportation improvement within a determined design time span. Therefore, a transportation improvement within this planning area would have no effect upon the long-term productivity. The only effect that a transportation improvement could have is on the short-term productivity. The short-term productivity effect of the Illinois Route 43 (Harlem Avenue) highway improvement would be a continued urbani- zation of the area. This proposed highway improvement will affect a safer and more efficient transportation of people, goods, and services, and this in turn will provide the area with an attractiveness for continuing residential and commercial development. VI. ANY IRREVERSIBLE AND IRRETRIEVABLE COMMITMENTS OF RESOURCES WHICH WOULD BE INVOLVED IN THE PROPOSED ACTION SHOULD IT BE IMPLEMENTED The proposed Harlem Avenue improvement will, for the most part, require land which is already dedicated to highway use. The commitment of resources involved is only a diminishing effect that can be negated by maintaining minimum disruption to the acquired Cook County Forest Preserve District property through the use of timely and appropriate plantings of land- scape materials. The Forest Preserve District is committed to the preservation and protection of natural resources, the proposed acquisition of right-of-way will not destroy this commitment. The Depart- ment of Transportation will not allow its right-of-way taking to be committed to any other purpose than slope redressing and planting of flora materials consistent with those presently under the protection of the Forest Preserve. The resource of soil will be protected from erosion in the slope design and further enhanced and stabilized by the landscape plantings. The acquisition of the right-of-way will not reduce the total amount of land under Forest Preserve status, because suitable land replacement will be provided. VII. PLANNING AND MEASURES TAKEN AND PROPOSED TO MINIMIZE HARM A. Water pollution, drainage and flooding: Drainage for this highway is in accordance with the provisions and limitations established by the General Assembly of the State of Illinois, as contained in the Illinois Revised Statutes. Based upon the enactment of this legislation, the use of highway funds for other than highway purposes is restricted. Highway drainage is designed to prevent the accumulation and retention of water on and by the highway. Also, in accordance with the State Laws governing the expenditure of highway funds, improvements to the drainage of areas outside of the right-of-way are not considered unless the highway will benefit thereby. Highway right- of-way does nothing to change the natural system of drainage or increase the flow to the downstream channel that the Channel is not required to accept by State law. In the improvement of this highway, present drainage patterns will be maintained, erosion and sedimentation due to roadway drainage slopes, curb and gutter, and by seeding or sodding of exposed surfaces. The improvement will utilize a storm sewer drainage system with sedimentation basins, and curb and gutter along its entire length. B. Air pollution and erosion: Air pollution resulting from highway construction will be limited. The contractors will utilize non-burning techniques for disposal of brush and timber, and dust will be kept at a minimum by sprinkling with water trucks. Contractors will be limited to the area of raw, erodible earth exposed at any given time. Construction of silt basins and timely plantings for erosion control will also be utilized. Wherever profile grade changes are necessary contour grading will be utilized within the highway right-of-way to maintain erosion control. As many trees as possible, along the right-of-way, will be protected. Appropriate use of plantings will be utilized to enhance the right-of-way and to blend with the surrounding vegetation. On this pro- ject plantings will be used for ground cover and erosion control of side slopes, while certain types of shrubs and trees will be used for esthetic effects. VIII. MEASURES TO MINIMIZE HARM TO SECTION 4(f) LANDS The Section 4(f) land involved with the Harlem Avenue improvement lies between 131st Street and 143rd Street. The 4(f) land involved is Forest Preserve District pro- perty of Cook County, Tinley Creek Division. The approximate three (3) acres required for the Harlem Avenue improvement will be used to provide backslopes for the the roadway cross-section. The diminishing aspects of construction to provide backslopes cannot be denied, but such diminishing aspects will be rectified by the use of timely plantings of proper vegetation in landscaping the project. There will be no destruction or derogation of the forest preserve property by this improvement. Landscaping will negate any potential diminishing aspects of the improvement, and the removal of old field entrances along the highway which lead onto forest preserve property will prevent any indiscriminate and uncontrolled access by the public. The removal of the field entrances was requested by the Forest Preserve District. As a further measure to minimize harm to the Forest Preserve property, a land exchange was initiated. The Forest Preserve District of Cook County has concurred that the 4.80 acre parcel of their Plat No. 559, is a fair land exchange for the approximate 3.0 acres needed for highway right-of-way. (See Appendix A , Exhibit 9 and Appendix B - Section 4(f)) A landscape plan is in preparation and will be coordinated with the Cook County Forest Preserve District. The land- scaping plan will be to the satisfaction of the Forest Pre- serve District prior to construction in the Section 4(f) involvement area between 131st and 143rd Streets. COMMENTS RECEIVED ON THE DRAFT DESIGN ENVIRONMENTAL/SECTION IX. 4(f) STATEMENT WITH A DISCUSSION OF THE COMMENTS AND SUGGESTIONS Public Hearing Comments Α. The public hearing was held on 19 March, 1970 at the Palos East School Auditorium. There were no specific environmental comments so directed at the public hearing. 1. Residential disruption At the public hearing all six alignment variations were formally presented. The response of those citizens attending the hearing was unanimously in favor of either Variation "A" or "A-1". The resi- dents of Palos Heights were determined and reaffirmed their resistance to relocating Harlem Avenue through the residential areas in order to avoid the shopping area. The City officials and the merchants affirmed that they would accept Variation "A-l", if the proposed frontage roads could be coordinated with a parking provision along the frontage of the stores between 122nd and 124th Streets. With the cooperation of the city and consideration of parking, a recommended plan was formulated. The final result of discussion and cooperation on the various alternates provided a plan with a frontage road parking area and a roadway profile very near the existing grade of the present facility. All of the original controversial impacts have been resolved. The improvement will follow the alignment described in Variation "A". Flooding Problems A major area of concern expressed by citizens at the public hearing involved the flooding problem in the area of 127th Street. The major water course in this is Navajo Creek. The pattern of flooding for a major portion of the creek's length indicates that the entire channel cross section, culverts and underground conduits, may be inadequate to handle the storm runoff from the watershed. The pattern of flooding upstream and downstream shows every indication that downstream restric- tions are the probable cause of the flooding problem in the area. The pattern of flooding does not indicate whether or not any particular culverts are adequate or inadequate. The existing drainage structure for Navajo Creek at Harlem Avenue is a 25 foot by 7 foot box cul- vert. The capacity of the culvert is sufficient to handle the watershed runoff, based on current culvert design criteria. Inspection of the cul- vert showed that it is almost completely filled with silt which further supports evidence that downstream conditions are restricting the flow of water. When downstream restrictions occur water is ponded, its velocity reduces toward zero and deposition of suspended particles and silt occurs. A culvert will be provided at 127th Street near Harlem Avenue. The culvert will have sufficient capacity to handle the design runoff. Drainage for Harlem Avenue is in accordance with the provisions and limitations established by the General Assembly of the State of Illinois, as contained in the Illinois Revised Statutes. WASHER MOV 1 Dear Mr. Ziefjewski: This is in response to your letter of August 23 concerning the draft environmental/Section 4(f) statement for the proposed improvement of Harlem Avenue (Federal-Aid Route 42) from 119th to 143rd Streets in Palos Heights, Cook County, Illinois. The draft environmental/Section 4(f) statement reveals that the proposed improvement will necessitate the acquisition of about three acres from the Forest Preserve District lands. Although it appears that there are no feasible or prudent alternatives to the proposed alignment, we think that the taking of three acres of Forest Preserve land is a setback to the goals and objectives of the Cook County Forest Preserve Commission. It should be noted that the Forest Preserve District is the recipient of considerable assistance from the Land and Water Conservation Fund and has applications pending for assistance in the purchase of additional recreation and open space lands in Cook County. The taking of this land, without reasonable substitution, would be inconsistent with the actions taken by this Department in furtherance of the Land and Water Conservation Fund. Accordingly, we recommend that efforts be made by the State to replace the park land with land of reasonably equivalent park usefulness and location. The District Commissioners should make this equality determination with respect to any proposed replacement land. Should land replacement not be possible, we would recommend that the Forest Preserve District be compensated according to

chunks.title: Section 4(f) Compliance, Mitigation Measures, and Environmental Concerns
chunks.description: This excerpt addresses Section 4(f) requirements for park land impacts, including compensation mechanisms, landscaping coordination with the Cook County Forest Preserve District, and replacement of three acres of parkland. It also identifies gaps in the draft EIS regarding hydrologic, geologic, and historic environmental aspects that require further analysis and discussion in the final statement.
chunks.text:
the replacement value of the lands being taken. Only if the
appraisal is based on replacement value, assuming such is allow-
able by law, would we consider that the District is being justly
compensated for the land being taken.
Should the final decision be to compensate the District, provision
should be made for the District to earmark such funds for capital
park and recreation purposes, i.e., land acquisition, new facilities,
etc. 1F
The draft environmental/Section 4(f) statement reflects that the
State will landscape the backslopes. We are told that, as yet,
the highway department has not discussed this matter with Forest
Preserve District officials, but that it is one in which they
have much interest in order to have a suitable screen between
the highway and the park to reduce noise and visual intrusions.
A landscaping plan, therefore, should be developed jointly with
and to the satisfaction of the Cook County Forest Preserve
District, such plan to be carried out as a part of the highway
project.
In summary, the final Section 4(f) submission should outline
(1) the planned means for replacing the three acres and (2) all
measures which will be taken to minimize harm.
With respect to other environmental matters, we find that the
draft statement fails to discuss certain hydrologic, geologic,
and historic aspects which are of concern.
In reference to the hydrologic aspects, the only problem that
might possibly affect the area's hydrologic system would be
the creation of a flood potential at the intersection of Navajo
Creek and Harlem Avenue. Careful consideration should be given
to the design of a waterway opening at this site. Information
concerning this site is available in Hydrologic Investigation
Atlas HA 145, "Floods in Palos Park Quadrangle, Northeastern
Illinois."
Since there was no geologic information included in the draft
statement, potential geologic problems cannot be assessed.
However, the types of environmental problems that may result
from local geologic conditions should be recognizable from
preconstruction investigations and should be within the range
of standard engineering practices. The final environmental/
Section 4(f) statement should include a discussion of any
anticipated problems.
There are no existing or potential units of the National Park
System in the vicinity of the proposed project, nor are there
any Registered Natural Landmarks or Registered Historic Landmarks.

The agencies comment on mitigation measures for Forest Preserve Land loss and request further discussion of replacement land or compensation options in the final statement.

Golterman, Chief Highway Engineer .Illinois Division of Highways .Cr - 2300 South Thirty-first Street V - Springfield, Illinois 62706 1014 1 1 1 ... Dear Mr. Golterman: lle ATTENTION: Mr. W. E. Baumann, Engineer of Design FA - 42 Draft Environmental/Section 4(f) Statement Subject: Illinois Project F-4() Section 3127-5 Ill-43 (Harlem Ave.), 119th St. to 143rd St. Cook County We have the following comments concerning the Draft Environmental/ Section 4(f) Statement for the above project. The final statement should be more clearly labeled and referred to as a "combined Environmental/Section 4(f) Statement". The environmental statement would benefit from editing to eliminate and/or condense verbose discussions in the draft regarding matters which are not directly pertinent...i.e., citation of State laws per- taining to location of FAI system, citation of various transportation planning goals, etc. The final statement would also be improved by providing cogent description of alternatives (including "alignment variations") rather than the somewhat questionable approach of indi- cating this is a case of accepting the 3C planning process or of not building the proposed facility. The Section 4(f) statement should include further information to offer a better understanding of the relationship of the proposed highway im- provement and the 4(f) lands and the extent of the impact. It should, for example, discuss provisions for compensating or replacing the 4(f) lands and improvements thereon, including the status of any agreements. A design alternative which should be discussed would be the elimination or minimization of the proposed median to minimize harm vs. its need for safety and/or operational benefits. Sincerely yours, CC: S.C. Ziejewski C. S. Monnier Attn: H.M. chunks.title: State Agency Comments on Environmental Impact Statements for Highway Projects
chunks.description: This excerpt contains official responses from two Illinois state agencies regarding environmental impact statements for highway projects dated August-September 1971. The Office of Manpower Development indicates no manpower-related concerns, while the Department of Business and Economic Development provides specific comments on a Route 47 project in Champaign County, recommending turn lanes near Lake of the Woods Park and correcting wildlife habitat assessments.

Dear Mr. Golterman:
Reference is made to our previous correspondence regarding review of
Environmental Impact Statements on various highway projects by members
of the Natural Resource Development Board.
The Natural Resource Development Board has the following comment on: FA Rte. 64 (Ill. Rte. 47) Sections 129, 136, 137 (W, RS) Champaign
County.
The Department of Conservation offers the following comments
relative to the foregoing named project:
Since the entrance to Lake of the Woods Park is off Route 47,
it is recommended that turn lanes be provided for safer entrance
onto and exiting from the State Route.
On page 6, Item (8), it is stated that present use of the
adjacent lands is not conducive to propagation of wildlife.
This, of course, is not the case since this is, for example,
in Illinois' best pheasant range. While the habitat would
be improved by an increase in and good interspersion of
woody cover and safe nesting areas, the pheasant is doing
surprisingly well in spite of intensive agriculture. Many
other forms of wildlife also are found throughout this
agricultural region.

The program, already researched and proved valuable, of
seeding road right-of-ways to a special brome-alfalfa mixture,
coupled with a program of delayed mowing, greatly increases
pheasant and other wildlife production. It is, therefore, recommended that on this project the alfalfa-brome seeding
mixture be used when seeding the right-of-way.
It is further recommended for this highway, as well as others,
in this section of Illinois, that special consideration be given
to a program of delayed mowing of the back slopes -- preferably
until August 1.
By treating the right-of-way of this new highway in the
foregoing manner, it actually can enhance conditions for
wildlife in this area. FA Rte. 413 Suppl. Freeway F-13 Mercer, Warren & McDonough Counties.
The Department of Conservation offers the following statement
in respect to the foregoing named project:
Within this extensive stretch of new 4-lane highway, numerous areas of high quality woody and herbaceous wildlife habitat
will be encountered. It will be necessary to destroy some of it in the process of constructing this highway. Every effort
should be made to avoid such areas whenever possible, and
where they cannot be by-passed, the area destroyed should
be held to a minimum. Much of the habitat destroyed can be
replaced with judicious plantings of trees, shrubs, crown
vetch, lespedeza, and grasses, such as brome, which are
favored for nesting. The limited and non-mowing program
will permit these right-of-ways, particularly the back-slopes
to be productive of wildlife.
In the case of stream and river crossings it is recommended
that the alignment and construction be such as to alleviate the
need for any channel straightening. Where straightening may
be mandatory, it is recommended that the cut-off oxbows be
left unfilled and that they not be used for deposition of spoil.
Some streams and rivers will be crossed where there is
substantial corridor of timber on one or both sides of the
waterway. Usually these corridors provide habitat for
Mr. Richard H. Golterman
Page 3
September 23, 1971
deer and other woodland wildlife. In such instances it is
recommended that bridge construction be such as to allow
for passage of animals under the bridge. This will reduce
the possibility of deer in particular crossing the freeway
and will decrease the frequency of car-wildlife collisions. The statement in paragraph one, page 5, that "species of
birds and animals are not able to nest or breed in this type
of concentrated operation" (meaning agricultural operations)
is not true. Wildlife does reproduce throughout the agricultural
regions of Illinois as is evidence by the quality hunting we have
for many game species. In this 75-mile stretch of 4-lane high-
way there will be considerable destruction of wildlife habitat
which does produce numerous species of all forms of Illinois
fauna.
The limited mowing referred to on page 9 will help to offset
losses and, in some areas, may increase the area available
for safe nesting and rearing of young.

Dear Mr. Ziejewski: We have reviewed the draft environmental impact statement on the above referenced project and offer the following comments. The Regional Open Space Plan makes a specific recommendation that: "Existing Open Space areas should be protected from encroachment by highways, schools and other public facilities. Only under very special conditions should such use of open space be allowed." The Plan further suggests that conversion of open space to other uses be approved if: "minimal open space area is taken: Equivalent open space area is provided elsewhere and simultaneously in the same general area; no other sites are available and re-development is not feasible; residents of the community approve of such action and highest design standards and creaivity are utilized." In order for the Harlem Avenue improvement to be consistent with the regional Open Space Plan the above requirements should be met (to the extent that they are applicable to this project) and the Cook County Forest Preserve District would have to be satisfied with the proposal and replacement property. chunks.description: The Forest Preserve District of Cook County responds to a request from the Division of Highways to acquire 3 acres of preserve land for Route 42 improvements, objecting to characterizing the land as having little or no value. The District argues that any erosion of forest preserve holdings for non-open space uses is significant given regional open space deficits and conflicts with the District's mandate to protect natural resources.

Dear Mr. Ziejevski:
I have been asked to respond to your recent letter requesting
a statement to the "significance" of your proposed Right of Way
taking.
Although the District will probably accede to the Division of
Highway's request for the land deemed essential for this improvement,
we have difficulty in accepting the terminology required for your
Federal Administrative review. We consider the proposal that the
District "determine" that the three acres to be removed from Forest
Preserve for highway use is of "little or no value" in conflict with
our long standing land policy. Such a statement is antithesis to the
Forest Preserve law which purpose is to create Forest Preserves for
the purpose of protecting and preserving the flora, fauna, and scenic
beauties within such District and to restore, restock, protect, and
preserve the natural forest and said lands together with thier flora
and fauna as nearly as may be in their natural state and condition
for the purpose of the education, pleasure, and recreation of the
public.
The recently published Northeastern Illinois Planning Commission's
Open Space Report cites the need for 58,000 additional acres of open
space in Cook County. Erosion of our holdings for non-open space use is significant however small the acreage may appear to be in relation-
ship to the whole.
EXHIBIT - 7
June 17, 1971
Mr. Ziejewski In this instance the lands to be acquired were not acquired
with H.U.D. or B.O.R. funds and therefore the federal mandate to
replace the land with land of equal use and value does not apply;
making this strictly a cash transaction. It is of course, the
District's prerogative to use these funds for replacement purposes,
however, administrative cost of handling such a replacement is no-
where met in the compensation for the lands lost. In effect there-
fore, we end up subsidizing a Highway activity.
As to the effect of the Right of Way taking to the remaining
property in the tract, the value of the tract is diminished for
the preservation of the natural resources that we have jurisdiction
and responsibility to protect.
The Forest Preserve District in the past has cooperated with
the Highway Department and will attempt to cooperate in the future
when the betterment of the people of Cook County is served.
Very truly yours
Richard Buck
Landscape Architect

SECTION 4(f) FINAL
The State of Illinois, Department of Transportation, Divi-
sion of Highways, is requesting approval of the proposed
Federal-Aid Highway Project F-4 ( ), in Cook County, Illinois.
The project provides for the widening of Federal-Aid Primary
Route 42 (Illinois Route 43) locally known and referred to as
Harlem Avenue, from 119th Street to 143rd Street.
The improvement will require some 3.0 acres right-of-way
acquisition from the Cook County Forest Preserve District
along a portion of its length and accordingly, approval of the
project is prohibited by Section 4(f) of the DOT Act and
Section 138 of Title 23, U.S. Code, unless----
(1) there is no feasible and prudent alternative
to the use of such land, and
(2) such program includes all possible planning to minimize harm resulting from such use.
This statement, therefore, will set forth my Final Design
pursuant to Section 4 (f) of the Department of Transporta-
tion Act and Section 138 of Title 23, U.S. Code.
DESCRIPTION OF PROPOSED HIGHWAY IMPROVEMENT AND ITS SUR-
I.
ROUNDINGS
EXISTING FACILITY
Harlem Avenue is a major north-south arterial highway tra-
versing and serving the entire length of the Chicago Metro-
politan Area. As part of its five year program, the Illinois
Division of Highways proposes to improve and upgrade the traffic
carrying capabilities of this roadway from 119th Street to
143rd Street. (See Exhibits 1, 2, 5 & 6)
The existing roadway consists of two lanes, one eleven (11)
foot lane in each direction. The shoulders are gravel and
earthen. The existing drainage is earthen ditch. The ex-
isting highway right-of-way is 66 feet from 135th Street,
south, to 143rd Street and 100 feet, north of 135th Street.
Existing land use along Harlem Avenue varies from public land
on the south to residential and strip commercial on the north.
The Cook County Forest Preserve District owns land on both
sides of Harlem Avenue from 131st Street south to 143rd
Street and extending west to approximately 76th Avenue and
east to Central Avenue. The Exisiting Forest Preserve Holdings
consists of approximately 5,280 acres. The intersection at
143rd Street is surrounded on all sides by forest preserve
property. Residential development extends north from 131st
Street to 124th Street and from 121st Street to 119th Street.
Commercial development is centered around the 127th Street
intersection and intense strip commercial development extends
along Harlem Avenue from 124th Street to 121st Street. (See Exhibit 3)
PROPOSED FACILITY
The proposed improvement will consist of four lanes, two
twelve (12) foot lanes in each direction, separated by a
sixteen (16) foot median. The roadway will have curb and
gutter throughout and drainage will be provided by a closed
storm sewer system. The improvement of Harlem Avenue will
require 100 feet of right-of-way from 135th Street south to
143rd Street and no additional right-of-way north of 135th
Street. (See Exhibit 4)
Harlem Avenue from 143rd Street south has been constructed
as a four land divided facility with a sixteen foot median.
Harlem Avenue north of 119th Street is presently under de-
sign study for improvement. A new bridge has been constructed
across the Calumet-Sag Channel with four lanes and a median.
The proposed improvement will be constructed along the cen- terline of the existing facility. The improvement, as pro- posed, will require some right-of-way acquisition from the
Cook County Forest Preserve District. The improvement will
require about three (3) acres from an existing forest pre- serve holding, in this area, of 5,280 acres. (See Exhibit 5)
DESCRIPTION OF SECTION 4 (f) LAND
A. Size:
The total land area of the Cook County Forest Preserve
District property involved in approximately 5,280 acres.
(See Exhibit 8)
В.
Type:
Recreational, nature study, and local historical inter-
est in relationship to the early settlers of the area.
C. Available Activities:
6. Horseback riding
l. Fishing
7. Nature study
2. Hiking Picnicking
8. Bird watching
9. Field atheletics
4. Bicycling
Camping (restricted)
Facilities:
D.
Auto parking lots
Drinking water Sanitary ficilities Picnic tables Picnic stoves
Open areas or meadows for recreational play (field atheletics) Concrete dance platforms Shelters
Forest Preserve District Headquaters of the Tin- ley Creek Division.
E.
Usage:
Specific, accurate, figures on the number of users for
each activity is not available.
F. Patronage:
Patronage is local and regional.
.G. Relationship to other similarly used lands in the
vicinity:
There are other Forest Preserve lands of the Tinley
Creek Division approximately 3 miles southeast of the
property involved with this improvement. There is also
Forest Preserve District land of the Palos & Sag Valley
Divisions and Thorn Creek Division approximately five
miles southeast and northwest of the Tinley Creek
Division (See Exhibit 9).
Access:
Η.
Access to the forest preserve is by vehicle and pedes-
trian.
Ownership:
I.
The property belongs to the Forest Preserve District
of Cook County, Illinois (Tinley Creek Division).
J. Deed restrictions or reversionary clauses:
There are no deed restrictions or reversionary clauses
involved in the three (3) acres of land required for the
improvement of Harlem Avenue. There are no B.O.R. or
H.U.D. Project funds involved with this Section 4 (f)
property.
<u>Determination of significance:</u>
Κ.
The determination of significance of the Section 4 (f)
land and the proposed acquisition of approximately 3.0
acres for the Harlem Avenue improvement is contained in
Exhibit 7 & 107
Unusual characteristics of the Section 4(f) land:
L.
There are no unusual characteristics of the Section 4
(f) land proposed for acquisition.
Consistency of location, community goals, objectives,
Μ.
and land use planning to use of Section 4 (f) land: The development of the proposed plan for improvement
has been coordinated with the Cook County Forest Preserve
District. Plans were submitted for their review and com-
ments. Correspondence received from them suggested that certain old farm field entrances be eliminated in order
to restrict unauthorized access to their property. We
will comply to their request. (See Exhibit 7)
Funds used in Forest Preserve Development:
N.
The Forest Pres 've District has indicated that no H.U.D.
or B.O.R. fund are associated with their property imvolved
in this improvement. (See Exhibit 7)
ANY PROBABLE ADVERSE ENVIRONMENTAL EFFECTS WHICH CANNOT BE
II.
AVOIDED SHOULD THE PROPOSAL BE IMPLEMENTED.
The location of the Section 4 (f) land involved with the
improvement of Harlem Avenue lies between 131st Street on
the north and 143rd Street on the south, a distance of approx-
imately one and one-half (1 1/2) miles in length. The loca-
tion is Township 37 North, Range 12 and 13 East, Sections:
36 and 31, Palos and Worth, Townships respectively; and
Township 36 North, Range 12 and 13 East, Sections: 1 and 6,
Orland and Bremen Townships respectively in Cook County,
Illinois.
The acquisition of Section 4 (f) land involves about 17
feet of additional right-of-way width each side for a total
amount of approximately three (3.0) acres. (See Exhibit 5,
for detailed mapping of Section 4 (f) involvement.)
There are no recreational or physical facilities affected by
the acquisition of this land.
There are no probable increase or decrease of physical effects
Sign of Spirit
on the users of the Section 4 (f) land involved in acquisi-
tion since there are no physical or recreational facilities
directly attributed to or available on the land to be acquired.
There will be no effect on authorized pedestrian or vehic-
ular access to the Forest Preserve District property or
Tinley Creek Division Headquarters. At the request of the
Forest Preserve District old farm and vehicle entrances will
be eliminated in order to restrict unauthorized access to
their property.
The only probable adverse environmental effect to the ac-
quisition of the 3.0 acres of Forest Preserve land would be
the diminishing aspects of its use. Since the land to be
acquired will not be used for the roadway but for sideslopes
and backslopes its evident that there will not be total
destruction of the land. There will be diminishing aspects
of the preservation of natural resources that the Forest
Preserve District has the jurisdiction and responsibility to protect.
<u>ALTERNATIVES</u>
III.
A. Not to improve Harlem Avenue:
This alternative would not be consistent with the goals
and objectives of National, State and Regional Transpor-
tation agencies through their continuing process of com-
prehensive transportation planning as set forth in the
Federal-Aid Highway Act of 1962. The elimination of the
improvement would further leave an abyss in the area
transportation system which would create drastic detri-
mental and significant adverse effects of congestion,
accidents, etc. along Harlem Avenue and in the other seg-
ments and links of the system.
To improve Harlem Avenue along the existing alignment:
В.
To improve Harlem Avenue along the existing alignment not
only meets the goals and objectives of providing a balanced
transportation system but is the most feasible and economic-
al alignment variation.
The entire existing highway right-of-way, already committed
to highway use, is available for the improvement.
Minimal land from the Forest Preserve District will be
required, approximately 3.0 acres.
Variation of the alignment to miss all forest preserve
land or acquire new right-of-way through the forest pre-
serve land and still connect with the established termini
at 143rd Street would not be feasible, predent or econo-
mical. The forest Preserve lands are bounded by 131st
Street on the north, 151st Street on the south, approxi-
mately 76th Avenue on the west, and Central Avenue on the
east. There is no way that any variations east or west
of existing Harlem Avenue could minimize the environmental
impact. In essence any alignment variations would cause
significant adverse effects.
Any alignment variation west of the existing roadway
could cost up to 3.5 million dollars more and any variation
east of the roadway could cost up to 5.0 million dollars
more than the proposed improvement which utilizes only
about 3.0 acres of Section 4(f) land for sideslopes and
backslopes.
Recommendation
c.
Based on the additional cost, the propable significant
adverse environmental effects on the forest preserve
property for any alignment variations east of west, and
the area transportation system of not improving Harlem
Avenue; we find that the improvement along the existing
alignment is the only feasible and prudent alternative.
MEASURES TO MINIMIZE HARM TO SECTION 4(f) LAND
IV.
The agency responsible for the acquisition of the three
Α.
(3.0) acres of Forest Preserve land will be the Illinois
Department of Transportation.
B. Provisions for compensating and replacement of the Section

chunks.title: Section 4(f) Land Use Determination and Mitigation for Harlem Avenue Improvement
chunks.description: This section documents the Illinois Department of Transportation's determination regarding the use of Cook County Forest Preserve District property for the Harlem Avenue improvement project. It describes mitigation measures including a proposed land exchange, landscaping plans to minimize environmental disruption, and coordination with the Forest Preserve District to replace Section 4(f) land and protect natural resources.
chunks.text:
4(f) land have been accomplished. The Forest Preserve
District of Cook County has agreed to a proposed land
exchange involving a 4.80 acre parcel of their Plat No.
are a
559 for the Harlem Avenue improvement. They agree that
this is a fair land exchange. (See Forest Preserve
District letter 30 December 1971, Exhibit .)
C. The forest preserve property adjacent to Harlem Avenue
is generally undeveloped at present, however any public
land in a growing metropolitan area is considered to
be of local and regional significance. The majority
of the land to be acquired will not be used for the road-
way itself but rather for the sideslopes. As many trees
as technically and feasibly possible will be saved or
protected along the right-of-way and blended with the
surrounding and existing vegetation. On this project
plantings will be used for ground cover and erosion
control of sideslopes, while certain types of shrubs
and trees will be used for aesthetic effects. All
appropriate landscaping techniques will be utilized to
negate any diminishing aspects of the preservation of
natural resources that the forest preserve has the
jurisdiction and responsibility to protect.
D. The net result of this improvement will permit the up-
grading of Harlem Avenue while minimizing the disruption
and diminishing aspects of the facility to the Cook
County Forest Preserve District property. (See attached
correspondence with Cook County Forest Preserve District
Exhibits 7 & ...
A landscape plan is in preparation and will be coordi-
nated with the Cook County Forest Preserve District.
The landscaping plan will be to the satisfaction of the
Forest Preserve District prior to construction in the
Section 4(f) involvement area between 131st Street
and 143rd Streets.
V.
DETERMINATION
It is my determination that there are no feasible, econo-
mical and prudent alternatives to the use of the Cook
County Forest Preserve District property and that the
program for improvement of Harlem Avenue (FAP Route 42)
Section: 3127-5, Cook County, Illinois, from 119th Street
to 143rd Street includes all possible planning and pro-
visions for replacing Section 4(f) land to minimize harm
resulting from the use of the property by the Illinois
Department of Transportation.

Janura, GENERAL SUPERINTENDENT June 17, 1971 Mr. Sigmund C. Ziejewski Regional Transportation Engineer Division of Highways Room-907 300 North State Street Attentibn - Richard L. Garrity RE: F.A. Route 42, Section 3127-5 Cook County, Harlem Avenue 119th Street to 143rd Street Dear Mr. Ziejevski: I have been asked to respond to your recent letter requesting a statement to the "significance" of your proposed Right of Way taking. Although the District will probably accede to the Division of Highway's request for the land deemed essential for this improvement, we have difficulty in accepting the terminology required for your Federal Administrative review. We consider the proposal that the District "determine" that the three acres to be removed from Forest Preserve for highway use is of "little or no value" in conflict with our long standing land policy. Such a statement is antithesis to the Forest Preserve law which purpose is to create Forest Preserves for the purpose of protecting and preserving the flora, fauna, and scenic beauties within such District and to restore, restock, protect, and preserve the natural forest and said lands together with thier flora and fauna as nearly as may be in their natural state and condition for the purpose of the education, pleasure, and recreation of the public. The recently published Northeastern Illinois Planning Commission's Open Space Report cites the need for 58,000 additional acres of open space in Cook County. Erosion of our holdings for non-open space use is significant however small the acreage may appear to be in relation- ship to the whole. EXHIBIT - 7 June 17, 1971 Mr. Ziejewski In this instance the lands to be acquired were not acquired with H.U.D. or B.O.R. funds and therefore the federal mandate to replace the land with land of equal use and value does not apply; making this strictly a cash transaction. It is of course, the District's prerogative to use these funds for replacement purposes, however, administrative cost of handling such a replacement is no- where met in the compensation for the lands lost. In effect there- fore, we end up subsidizing a Highway activity. As to the effect of the Right of Way taking to the remaining property in the tract, the value of the tract is diminished for the preservation of the natural resources that we have jurisdiction and responsibility to protect. The Forest Preserve District in the past has cooperated with the Highway Department and will attempt to cooperate in the future when the betterment of the people of Cook County is served. Very truly yours Richard Buck Landscape Architect RB: mo EXHIBIT - 7 Points of Interest Forest Preserve District of Cook County Lake maintained to furnish good fishing.
